headings.text: What the Ocean Is Made Of
Fig. 2 — Plastic Flow Sankey: Sources → Depth Zones
Fig. 6 — Plastic Mass by Ocean Depth Zone

paragraphs.text: A polymer cartography of the open sea — tracing 11 million metric tons of plastic from the moment they leave the shore to the gyres, fibres, and microparticles that now define modern marine geology.
From single-use packaging to ghost gear, every tide carries a synthetic ledger. This issue maps the inputs, the flows, and the polymer chemistry of an ocean increasingly composed of human residue.
MT · METRIC TONSPPM · PARTICLES / M³µM · MICROMETRE

Five subtropical gyres — vast rotational currents driven by the Coriolis effect — concentrate buoyant debris into convergence zones the size of subcontinents. The North Pacific accumulation alone now contains an estimated 79,000 MT of floating plastic spread across 1.6 million km².
Equivalent to a garbage truck of plastic discharged every 60 seconds. Projected to triple by 2040 under business-as-usual scenarios.

Ten dominant source streams feed three vertical destinations — surface waters, water column, and seabed sediments. Single-use packaging dominates input volume, while abandoned fishing gear contributes disproportionately to long-lived, large-format debris.
Derelict fishing nets and lines account for nearly half of the Great Pacific Garbage Patch by mass, despite contributing only 18% of total inflow.
Released during synthetic textile laundering — most pass through municipal filtration unimpeded.

PET and HDPE together account for over half of all surface debris by mass. Both polymers are buoyant in seawater (density < 1.025 g/cm³), favouring long-distance gyre transport before degradation begins.
A figure exceeding the count of stars in the Milky Way by a factor of 500. Of these, an estimated 236,000 MT are particles smaller than 5 mm.

Under business-as-usual, annual ocean inflow reaches 29 million MT by 2040 — and cumulative ocean stock surpasses 600 million MT. The proposed UN Global Plastics Treaty (2025) aims to cap production at 2020 levels and could reduce projected inflow by 80%.
An interactive infographic journal translating NOAA and peer-reviewed ocean science into dense, visually rich data stories.

visibleText: Deep Current Issue 07 Ocean Health Coral Systems Plastic Tides Temperature Rise Action Atlas Volume III · Pollution Atlas Plastic Tides A polymer cartography of the open sea — tracing 11 million metric tons of plastic from the moment they leave the shore to the gyres, fibres, and microparticles that now define modern marine geology. From single-use packaging to ghost gear, every tide carries a synthetic ledger. This issue maps the inputs, the flows, and the polymer chemistry of an ocean increasingly composed of human residue. Published 2024 · 11 · 04 Dataset NOAA · OSPAR · GESAMP Reading 14 min Page Sections Five Garbage Gyres Source Flow Polymer Breakdown Microparticle Counts Depth Distribution Annual Ledger Depth Scale 0 M · SURFACE 200 · PHOTIC 1000 · MESO 4000 · ABYSSAL 11K · HADAL Data Key Single-use packaging Fishing & ghost gear Microfibres / textile Industrial pellets PET / clear polymer Degraded fragments Units MT · METRIC TONS PPM · PARTICLES / M³ µM · MICROMETRE § 01 / Gyre Mass Atlas Five Spinning Archives Updated · Q3 2024 Five subtropical gyres — vast rotational currents driven by the Coriolis effect — concentrate buoyant debris into convergence zones the size of subcontinents. The North Pacific accumulation alone now contains an estimated 79,000 MT of floating plastic spread across 1.6 million km² . Fig. 1 — Isometric Gyre Mass Cartogram Surface debris density · scaled by mass SVG · viewBox 0 0 840 420 N. PACIFIC 79,000 MT S. PACIFIC 21,000 MT N. ATLANTIC 42,000 MT S. ATLANTIC 12,500 MT INDIAN 28,500 MT ~3,000 KM Bubble radii are scaled proportional to estimated floating-plastic mass per gyre. Convergence rotation indicated by spiral path; embedded marker glyphs represent representative debris categories (bottle, pellet, net fragment). Source · NOAA Marine Debris Program (2024); Lebreton et al., Scientific Reports N. Pacific 79K MT · Largest gyre N. Atlantic 42K MT · Sargasso zone Indian 28.5K MT · Bay of Bengal S. Pacific 21K MT · Chilean coast S. Atlantic 12.5K MT · Brazilian rim 11 million MT Annual ocean inflow Equivalent to a garbage truck of plastic discharged every 60 seconds . Projected to triple by 2040 under business-as-usual scenarios. § 02 / Source Provenance Where the Plastic Comes From Sankey · 10 → 3 Ten dominant source streams feed three vertical destinations — surface waters, water column, and seabed sediments. Single-use packaging dominates input volume, while abandoned fishing gear contributes disproportionately to long-lived, large-format debris. Fig. 2 — Plastic Flow Sankey: Sources → Depth Zones Ribbon width ∝ annual mass (MT) 2023 baseline PACKAGING 34% FISHING GEAR 18% MICROFIBRES 12% TYRE WEAR 10% BOTTLES / PET 8% PERSONAL CARE 5% AGRI FILM 4% CIGARETTE FILTERS 4% PELLETS / NURDLES 3% OTHER 2% SURFACE 0–200m · 22% 2.4 MT WATER COLUMN 200–2000m · 31% 3.4 MT SEDIMENT >2000m · 47% 5.2 MT SOURCE STREAM →→→ DEPTH ZONE TERMINUS Ribbon thickness preserves mass conservation across the diagram. Cigarette filters disproportionately weight surface destinations due to filter buoyancy; tyre-wear particles fast-sink to the water column. Source · Borrelle et al., Science (2020); Pew Charitable Trusts; IUCN 2023 Top 10 Source Contributions Packaging 34% Fishing Gear 18% Microfibres 12% Tyre Wear 10% PET Bottles 8% Personal Care 5% Agri Film 4% Cig. Filters 4% Pellets 3% Other 2% 640 k MT Ghost gear lost annually Derelict fishing nets and lines account for nearly half of the Great Pacific Garbage Patch by mass, despite contributing only 18% of total inflow. 35 % Microfibres of all microplastic Released during synthetic textile laundering — most pass through municipal filtration unimpeded. § 03 / Polymer Chemistry What the Ocean Is Made Of 5 polymer classes Fig. 3 — Marine Plastic Polymer Composition Mass fraction · sampled surface debris 2020–2024 N = 18,400 fragments PET · 32% BOTTLES · TEXTILES HDPE · 24% CAPS · CONTAINERS PP · 19% FISHING ROPE · CAPS PS · 14% OTHER · 11% 5 POLYMER CLASSES SURFACE SAMPLE PET Polyethylene terephthalate 32% HDPE High-density polyethylene 24% PP Polypropylene 19% PS Polystyrene 14% Other PVC, PUR, mixed 11% PET and HDPE together account for over half of all surface debris by mass. Both polymers are buoyant in seawater (density < 1.025 g/cm³), favouring long-distance gyre transport before degradation begins. Donut arcs are SVG path elements with explicit cartesian endpoints. Annotation spokes terminate in circle markers per the editorial style guide. Source · GESAMP Working Group 40; NOAA NCCOS sampling consortium § 04 / Particle Census The Microplastic Cloud 51 trillion particles 51T Microplastic particles · currently afloat A figure exceeding the count of stars in the Milky Way by a factor of 500. Of these, an estimated 236,000 MT are particles smaller than 5 mm . Fig. 4 — Particle Size Distribution log-bin µm spectrum 2024 <100µm 100-300 300-500 0.5-1mm 1-2mm 2-3mm 3-5mm >5mm 0 25% 50% 75% 100% REL. PARTICLE COUNT BY SIZE BIN Fig. 5 — Concentration by Region particles · m⁻³ · surface trawl PPM N.PAC GYRE 1.8M MEDITERRANEAN 1.25M N.ATL GYRE 920K INDIAN OCEAN 680K ARCTIC 220K SOUTHERN OCEAN 95K PARTICLES / KM² · SURFACE LAYER "Every cubic metre of surface seawater in the North Pacific now contains more plastic particles than zooplankton — a sixfold inversion of the trophic baseline since 1990." — Dr. Marcus Eriksen · 5 Gyres Institute § 05 / Vertical Distribution The Sinking Inventory 0 → 11,000 m Fig. 6 — Plastic Mass by Ocean Depth Zone Cross-section · stacked density Global average SURFACE · 0–200m 22% EPIPELAGIC · MIXED LAYER 14% MESOPELAGIC · 200–1000m 17% BATHYPELAGIC · 1000–4000m 26% ABYSSAL SEDIMENT · >4000m 21% Counter-intuitively, less than a quarter of marine plastic remains at the surface where it is visible. The majority is suspended in the water column or already entombed in deep-sea sediments — including in the Mariana Trench at 10,898 m . Source · NOAA OAR; Choy et al., Sci Reports (2019); Peng et al., 2020 § 06 / Annual Ledger The 2024 Plastic Balance Sheet FY 2024 · projected Production 460M MT global plastic produced Mismanaged 82M MT escapes waste systems Ocean Inflow 11M MT enters marine env. Africa 640,000 5.8% 1.41 ↑ rising Europe & Central Asia 410,000 3.7% 0.45 ↓ declining North America 260,000 2.4% 0.71 ↓ declining Open-Ocean (gear) 980,000 8.9% — ↑ rising 2040 Projected tripling year Under business-as-usual, annual ocean inflow reaches 29 million MT by 2040 — and cumulative ocean stock surpasses 600 million MT . The proposed UN Global Plastics Treaty (2025) aims to cap production at 2020 levels and could reduce projected inflow by 80%.
